title: Consumer & producer surplus
summary:
## The big idea Markets don't just set prices — they create *benefit*. When you'd happily have paid \$5 for a sandwich but it only costs \$3, you walk away \$2 better off. When a seller would have accepted \$2 but receives \$3, they gain \$1. Economists call these gains **consumer surplus** and **producer surplus**, and together they measure the **welfare** a market generates. This is the toolkit you'll use for the rest of the course to judge whether taxes, subsidies, price controls and monopolies make society better or worse off. ## Consumer surplus — the buyer's bonus **Consumer surplus is the difference between what consumers are *willing* to pay and what they *actually* pay.** The demand curve is really a **willingness-to-pay curve**: the first, keenest buyers value the good highly; later buyers value it less. But *everyone* pays the same market price. So every buyer except the very last one gets a bonus — they valued the good at more than they paid. Add all those bonuses up and you get the **area below the demand curve and above the price line**. A tiny worked example: three fans would pay \$30, \$20 and \$12 for a concert ticket priced at \$10. Their surpluses are \$20, \$10 and \$2 — a total consumer surplus of **\$32**. ## Producer surplus — the seller's bonus **Producer surplus is the difference between the price producers actually receive and the minimum they would have accepted.** The supply curve shows each seller's minimum acceptable price (roughly their marginal cost). Low-cost producers would have supplied even at low prices — but they receive the full market price, so they pocket the difference. Producer surplus is the **area above the supply curve and below the price line**.
**What each part means:** the market clears at price **P\*** and quantity **Q\***. The **red shaded triangle (CS)** sits *below the demand curve and above the price line* — the total bonus to buyers who would have paid more than P\*. The **yellow shaded triangle (PS)** sits *above the supply curve and below the price line* — the total bonus to sellers who would have accepted less than P\*. Together the two triangles are the **total (community) surplus** the market creates.
## Total surplus and allocative efficiency **Total (community) surplus = consumer surplus + producer surplus.** At the free-market equilibrium this combined area is as **large as it can possibly be** — every unit worth more to a buyer than it costs a seller gets produced, and no unit that costs more than it's worth gets produced. That is what economists mean by **allocative efficiency** (at Q\*, price = marginal cost). Push the market away from Q\* in *either* direction and some surplus simply vanishes — nobody gets it. That lost triangle of welfare is the **deadweight loss**. Taxes, price controls and monopoly all shrink output below Q\* and create one; you will draw that triangle again and again in Unit 3 and at A2. ## How surpluses change - **Price falls** (say supply shifts right after a good harvest) → consumer surplus **grows**: existing buyers pay less *and* new buyers join the market. - **Price rises** → consumer surplus shrinks; producer surplus usually grows on the units still sold. - **Elasticity shapes the areas**: a steep (inelastic) demand curve creates a tall consumer-surplus triangle — buyers who *really* value the good. Relative elasticities also decide **who loses more surplus when a tax is imposed** (the less elastic side bears more — the incidence result from the tax chapter). ## Applications you must be able to draw - **Indirect tax:** supply shifts up, price rises, quantity falls — CS and PS both shrink, the government takes a tax rectangle, and a **deadweight-loss triangle** remains between the old and new quantities. - **Maximum price** (below equilibrium): buyers lucky enough to get the good gain surplus, but the shortage means fewer units are traded — total surplus falls. - **Monopoly** (A2): restricting output raises price, **transferring** consumer surplus to the producer and creating deadweight loss. ## Why it matters Surplus analysis is the bridge from *describing* markets to *judging* them. 'Who gains, who loses, and does total welfare rise or fall?' is exactly the structure examiners want in evaluation — and shading the right areas on a diagram is the fastest way to show it.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Consumer surplus** = willingness to pay − price actually paid = area **below D, above P\***. - **Producer surplus** = price received − minimum acceptable = area **above S, below P\***. - **Total surplus = CS + PS**, **maximised at competitive equilibrium** → allocative efficiency (P = MC). - Moving output away from Q\* (tax, price control, monopoly) → **deadweight loss**. - Price ↓ → CS ↑; price ↑ → CS ↓ (and PS usually ↑ on remaining units). - Relative **elasticities** decide how surplus (and tax burden) is shared between buyers and sellers. - Always **shade and label** CS, PS and any deadweight loss on the diagram.

keyTerms.definition: The difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay; the area below demand and above price.
The difference between the price producers receive and the minimum they would accept; the area above supply and below price.
Consumer surplus plus producer surplus; maximised at competitive equilibrium.
When resources are allocated to maximise total welfare (price = marginal cost), with no deadweight loss.
The loss of total surplus when output is pushed away from the efficient equilibrium.
The net benefit society gains from the consumption and production of goods.

diagrams: **Surplus diagram:** standard D/S with equilibrium P* and Q*. Shade the triangle below D and above P* (consumer surplus) and the triangle above S and below P* (producer surplus).
**Effect of a price fall:** show supply shifting right, price falling, and the consumer-surplus triangle enlarging.
**Deadweight loss:** show an indirect tax shifting supply up; mark the smaller surpluses and the deadweight-loss triangle between the old and new quantities.
tips: Get the **areas right**: consumer surplus is *below demand, above price*; producer surplus is *above supply, below price*. Mislabelling loses marks.
State that **total surplus is maximised at competitive equilibrium** to explain allocative efficiency.
Link surplus changes to **elasticity** — who gains/loses more depends on relative elasticities of demand and supply.
Use surplus diagrams to **evaluate interventions** (taxes, price controls, monopoly) and identify **deadweight loss**.
Always **shade and label** the relevant areas on the diagram rather than just describing them in words.

revisionNotes:
# Consumer & producer surplus — quick revision

**The two areas**
- **Consumer surplus** = what buyers were willing to pay − what they paid → area **below demand, above price**.
- **Producer surplus** = price received − minimum acceptable → area **above supply, below price**.
- Demand curve = willingness-to-pay curve; supply curve = minimum-acceptable-price (marginal cost) curve.

**Welfare**
- **Total (community) surplus = CS + PS** — maximised at free-market equilibrium.
- That is **allocative efficiency**: P = MC at Q\*; no deadweight loss.
- Any move away from Q\* destroys surplus → **deadweight loss triangle**.

**Changes**
- Price falls → CS rises (cheaper for existing buyers + new buyers enter).
- Price rises → CS falls; PS rises on units still traded.
- **Elasticities** decide the split: the less elastic side gains/loses more surplus (tax incidence link).

**Applications (be ready to draw)**
- Indirect tax → smaller CS & PS, tax revenue rectangle, deadweight loss.
- Maximum price → shortage, fewer trades, lost surplus.
- Monopoly (A2) → CS transferred to producer + deadweight loss.

**Exam habit**
- Shade and label the areas; don't just describe them in words.
questions:
# Practice questions — Consumer & producer surplus **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** Consumer surplus is best defined as: - A — the total amount consumers spend on a good - B — the difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay - C — the difference between price and the cost of production - D — the area above the supply curve and below the price
▸ Show answer

B. Consumer surplus is willingness to pay minus actual payment — the area below the demand curve and above the price. C and D describe producer surplus.

**2.** The market price of a good rises while the demand curve stays unchanged. Consumer surplus will: - A — rise - B — fall - C — stay the same - D — rise only if demand is elastic
▸ Show answer

B. A higher price squeezes the gap between willingness to pay and price paid, and prices some buyers out entirely — the CS triangle shrinks.

**3.** A consumer would pay up to \$25 for a book, a second up to \$18, and a third up to \$12. The market price is \$12. Total consumer surplus is: - A — \$55 - B — \$19 - C — \$31 - D — \$12
▸ Show answer

B. Surpluses are \$13 + \$6 + \$0 = \$19. The third buyer pays exactly their valuation, so gains nothing.

**4.** Total surplus in a competitive market is maximised when: - A — consumer surplus equals producer surplus - B — output is at the free-market equilibrium quantity - C — the government sets a maximum price - D — producers charge the highest price consumers will pay
▸ Show answer

B. At equilibrium every mutually beneficial trade happens and no wasteful ones do — allocative efficiency. The two surpluses need not be equal (A).

## Paper 2 style — data response > After an exceptional harvest, the market supply of mangoes in a region shifts sharply to the right. The wholesale price falls from \$4 to \$2.50 per kilo and the quantity traded rises from 800 to 1,100 tonnes. Growers complain about lower prices, while a consumer group celebrates. The government is considering a minimum price of \$4 to protect growers. **(a)** Define 'producer surplus'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain, with the aid of a diagram, what happens to consumer surplus when the supply of mangoes increases. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse how a minimum price of \$4 would affect consumer surplus, producer surplus and total welfare. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether the government should introduce the minimum price. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) The difference between the price producers receive and the minimum they would accept; area above supply, below price. [1+1]

(b) Supply shifts right → price falls to \$2.50 → CS grows: existing buyers pay \$1.50 less per kilo and new buyers enter (quantity rises to 1,100t). Show the larger triangle below D above the new price. [diagram 2, explanation 2]

(c) Minimum price above equilibrium → quantity traded falls (excess supply); CS shrinks (higher price, fewer units); PS may rise on units sold but surplus mangoes go unsold; total welfare falls — deadweight loss. [6]

(d) For: protects grower incomes, may stabilise supply long term. Against: deadweight loss, unsold surpluses (who buys/stores them?), consumers — often poorer households — pay more; better-targeted tools exist (direct income support). A clear judgement is required. [8]

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain how consumer surplus and producer surplus arise in a free market. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether the free-market equilibrium always maximises society's welfare. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Demand as willingness to pay, supply as minimum acceptable price; everyone trades at one price so both sides gain; identify both areas on a diagram.

(b) For: at equilibrium total surplus is maximised (P = MC, allocative efficiency). Against: this assumes no externalities, perfect information and competition — with market failure (pollution, merit goods, monopoly) equilibrium is not socially optimal; equity is also ignored (surplus to those able to pay). Judgement: maximises private welfare under strict assumptions only.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain, with the aid of a diagram, how an indirect tax affects consumer surplus and producer surplus. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether consumers or producers bear more of the burden of an indirect tax. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Tax shifts supply up by the tax; price to consumers rises, price received by sellers falls, quantity falls; CS and PS both shrink; government revenue rectangle; deadweight-loss triangle between old and new quantity.

(b) Depends on relative elasticities: the less elastic side bears more. Inelastic demand (fuel, tobacco) → consumers pay most; elastic demand/inelastic supply → producers absorb more. Use diagrams for both cases, discuss time horizons (demand more elastic long run), and conclude that no single answer fits all markets.
